# PaperPilot AI — Open-Source Readiness Audit, Roadmap & Issue Backlog

Generated by a full read of the codebase (backend, frontend, tests, CI,
docs) on 2026-07-23. `gh` CLI was not available in this environment, so
issues were **not** created on GitHub — they are written as individual
markdown files in this directory (`001-...md` through `067-...md`) for a
maintainer to paste into GitHub Issues (or import via a script using
`gh issue create --title ... --body-file ...` once `gh` is available and
authenticated).

This file is the index: the audit summary, the maturity report, the
milestone roadmap, the dependency graph, and the full issue table.

---

## Part 1 — Audit summary

This is a **well-run pre-1.0 project**, not a typical "vibe-coded" repo.
CLAUDE.md is unusually good: it documents real resolved incidents (not
aspirational fixes), the reasoning behind non-default config values (e.g.
the similarity-threshold calibration in §7), and where the two RAG stacks
join. The open-source scaffolding (CONTRIBUTING, CODE_OF_CONDUCT, SECURITY,
ROADMAP, CHANGELOG, issue templates, PR template, two CI workflows) is
already in place — this audit does not ask for any of that to be created,
only for the gaps within it to be closed.

**What's genuinely strong:**
- Provider-agnostic LLM selection (`llm/factory.py`) with real fallback
  behavior, not just config plumbing.
- The Tutor → Critic grounded-QA loop is live in production, bounded, and
  degrades gracefully (unaudited-but-flagged rather than a 500) when the
  critic itself is rate-limited.
- Retrieval design shows real empirical work: the similarity-threshold and
  lead-chunk mechanisms in `session.py` were tuned against measured
  cosine-similarity bands on a real paper, not guessed.
- `PDFDownloader` already distinguishes permanent vs. transient failures and
  enforces scheme allowlisting + streamed size caps.
- The offline-first test discipline (173 tests, stubs over mocks-of-mocks,
  `dependency_overrides` for FastAPI) is exactly the pattern a contributor
  should copy.

**What's genuinely missing, found by reading the code (not speculation):**
1. Three concrete, currently-live bugs (see #001, #002, #003 below) that
   nobody has hit yet only because the app has one user so far.
2. Zero frontend tests — the entire UI has no test infrastructure at all.
3. `tests/test_api.py` only covers 2 of the FastAPI backend's 9 endpoints.
4. A real, if narrow, SSRF gap: `PDFDownloader` allowlists URL *schemes*
   but not destination hosts — fine for a single local user, a genuine
   vulnerability the moment this is deployed for more than one person.
5. No Dependabot/CODEOWNERS/pre-commit — normal for a solo project, table
   stakes for one accepting outside PRs.
6. `.env.example` documents ~15 settings; `config.py` defines ~35 — most of
   the RAG tuning surface (chunk size, lead chunks, rerank, prefetch) is
   invisible to a new contributor unless they read the source.

None of these are architecture problems. The two-stack RAG design, the
Pydantic contract layer, and the Protocol-based provider pattern are sound
and should not change — every issue below works within that architecture.

---

## Part 2 — Maintainer report (Phase 7)

| Dimension | Score /10 | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Overall maturity** | 7/10 | Production-shaped code with real incident history behind it; missing is breadth (frontend tests, full endpoint coverage), not depth. |
| **Contributor onboarding** | 7/10 | CONTRIBUTING.md is excellent and current. Docked for `.env.example` drift and no "first PR" map — a newcomer has to read all of CLAUDE.md to find a safe starting point. |
| **Documentation** | 8/10 | CLAUDE.md is genuinely rare: it explains *why*, tracks resolved incidents, and is kept current (updated same day as the dark-mode PR). README is good. Missing: architecture diagram, API reference link, `.env.example` sync. |
| **Code quality** | 8/10 | Consistent conventions, real dependency injection, no dead code found, no TODOs left lying around. The few bugs found (path anchoring, workspace-name truncation) are subtle, not sloppy. |
| **Architecture** | 8/10 | The Stack A / Stack B join via `GroundedQAService` is a genuinely good design — most projects would have left two competing RAG paths. Protocol-based `SearchProvider` makes provider #3 (issue #020/#021) a small PR, not a rewrite. |
| **Test coverage** | 6/10 | Backend: 173 offline tests, strong on services/agents, thin on the API layer (2/9 endpoints) and `app/utils.py`. Frontend: 0/10 — no test runner installed at all. |
| **Security posture** | 6/10 | SECURITY.md is honest about the local-only threat model. The one real gap (SSRF via unrestricted destination host in `PDFDownloader`) is exactly the kind of thing that matters the moment someone deploys this for a lab or a team. |

**Biggest weaknesses:** no frontend test infrastructure at all; API test
coverage stops at chat/summary and misses workspace CRUD, search, and
paper-processing; three live bugs from path/naming edge cases; SSRF gap in
the PDF downloader.

**Biggest strengths:** CLAUDE.md's incident-log discipline; the grounded
QA/critic loop's graceful degradation; the LLM provider abstraction; the
overall absence of dead code or unaddressed TODOs.

**Quick wins (do this week, ~1 day total):** #001, #003, #006, #007, #008,
#009, #013, #025, #027, #028, #030, #031, #032, #051, #052, #057, #058 —
all Beginner/Easy, all independent, all mergeable same-day.

**Long-term vision:** the roadmap in ROADMAP.md/CLAUDE.md §11 is already
correct — wire the Planner, expose streaming, then build Comparison/
Roadmap/Quiz on `retrieve_across_papers`. This backlog operationalizes that
roadmap into mergeable units and fills the contributor-experience gap
around it.

**Estimated realistic stars if well-maintained:** **150–500 within a year**
of active maintenance with responsive PR review, assuming no viral moment.
Reasoning: the space (LLM research-paper assistants) is crowded but this
project's differentiators — an actual audited grounding loop instead of
"trust the LLM," and CLAUDE.md's unusually transparent incident log — are
real technical merit signals that attract engineers specifically (the
audience most likely to star and contribute), not virality. Reaching
1,000+ would need either a launch moment (Show HN / a demo video that
travels) or a genuinely novel feature (e.g. the Comparison/Roadmap/Quiz
trio, once shipped, is a differentiated pitch: "turns a reading list into a
study plan"). Nothing here is a ceiling — it's a floor conditional on
someone actually running the roadmap below.

**Top 10 issues to tackle first** (maximize quality-signal-per-hour for a
new maintainer trying to attract contributors): **#001, #026+#011 (paired),
#046, #041–#045 (API test coverage), #012, #004, #002, #052, #058.** These
fix the bugs a contributor would trip over first, close the most visible
test gap (frontend has none), close the security gap SECURITY.md already
flags as in-scope, and make the repo look actively maintained (badges,
Dependabot) within the first week.

---

## Part 3 — Roadmap (milestones)

Ordered by dependency; each milestone should be shippable and tagged.

### v0.2 — "Contributor-Ready Foundations" (38 issues)
Good-first-issues, documentation, CI/DX polish, and isolated bug fixes with
no cross-cutting risk. Goal: a newcomer can find a same-day PR and CI
looks like a maintained project.
`#003 #006 #007 #008 #009 #013 #025 #027 #028 #029 #030 #031 #032 #034 #037
#038 #039 #041 #042 #043 #047 #051 #052 #053 #054 #055 #056 #057 #058 #059
#060 #061 #062 #063 #064 #065 #066 #067`

### v0.3 — "Correctness, Security & Refactors" (16 issues)
The bugs and gaps that need more care: path handling, SQLite concurrency,
the SSRF gap, and the refactors that make v0.4's features cheaper to build.
`#001 #002 #004 #005 #010 #012 #033 #035 #036 #040 #044 #045 #046 #048 #049
#050`

### v0.4 — "Depth Features" (8 issues)
The last inert pieces of Stack A, plus the features that need v0.3's
groundwork (a real paper-by-id endpoint, WAL mode for concurrent writes).
`#011 #014 #015 #016 #017 #020 #021 #026`

### v1.0 — "Vision Complete" (5 issues)
Production-hardening for non-local deployment, and the three vision
features (Comparison, Roadmaps, Quizzes) that turn PaperPilot from "chat
with a paper" into "manage a reading program."
`#018 #019 #022 #023 #024`

---

## Part 4 — Dependency graph

Hard dependencies (the first must merge before the second can start):

```
Issue #011 (GET /api/papers/{id})
    ↓
Issue #026 (fix PaperSummary refresh/deep-link)

Issue #002 (fix workspace-name collision)
    ↓
Issue #048 (regression test for the collision)

Issue #012 (SSRF host allowlist)
    ↓
Issue #050 (PDFDownloader host-validation tests)

Issue #004 (WAL mode / busy_timeout)
    ↓
Issue #049 (SQLite concurrency test)
    ↓
Issue #017 (persist WorkspaceChatStore to SQLite — needs safe concurrent writes)

Issue #046 (bootstrap frontend test runner)
    ↓
(every future frontend-component test issue, including deeper coverage of
 #025–#040's fixes)

Issue #036 (extract shared ChatPanel component)
    ↓
Issues #032, #033, #038 (should land after the extraction, not before, to
avoid fixing the same bug twice in duplicated code)
```

Soft/recommended orderings (not blocking, but sequenced in ROADMAP.md's own
§11 for a reason):

```
Issue #016 (wire Planner agent)
    ~> Issues #022, #023, #024 (Comparison / Roadmap / Quiz)

Issue #018 (basic auth)
    ~> Issue #019 (rate limiting) — independently useful, but both matter
       most together for a non-local deployment
```

---

## How to use this backlog

1. Read `001-...md` onward — one file per issue, in the format requested
   (Background / Why it matters / Proposed solution / Acceptance criteria /
   Suggested files / Difficulty / Estimated time / Labels / Dependencies).
2. Create the labels referenced (`good first issue`, `bug`, `enhancement`,
   `documentation`, `performance`, `security`, `tests`, `frontend`,
   `backend`, `refactor`, `ui`) if they don't already exist.
3. Create four milestones: `v0.2`, `v0.3`, `v0.4`, `v1.0` (descriptions
   above), and paste each issue in with `gh issue create --title "..."
   --body-file 0NN-slug.md --label ... --milestone v0.2`, or by hand.
4. Link dependent issues in the issue body ("Depends on #002") once real
   issue numbers are assigned — the numbers in this backlog are backlog
   ordinals, not GitHub issue numbers.
